title: Speed Control
disclaimer: Training support only. Confirm local speed control procedures and aircraft performance assumptions with an instructor.
learning_objectives: Use speed assignment as a simple spacing tool.
Recognize aircraft speed limits from performance data.
Observe how speed changes alter route progress over time.
key_points: Speed control changes timing, not route geometry.
A speed instruction must stay within the aircraft performance envelope.
Speed is usually useful early; late speed control may have limited effect.

lesson_steps.title: Timing Tool
lesson_steps.body: Speed control helps adjust when aircraft reach a fix, crossing point, or sequence position.

lesson_steps.title: Watch The Gap
lesson_steps.body: Assign different speeds to two aircraft on the same or crossing routes, then watch their relative movement.

lesson_steps.title: Adjust Speed
script: Select another aircraft and keep its speed unchanged.
Compare route progress after a short period.

exercise.title: Slow One Aircraft And Observe Spacing
exercise.objective: Use one speed reduction to change traffic timing.
student_actions: Select an aircraft on a route with nearby traffic.
Observe whether the timing gap changes.
success_conditions: A valid speed instruction is accepted.
The student explains that speed changes timing rather than route.
debrief_prompts: Was the speed inside the aircraft performance range?
Did spacing improve quickly or slowly?
Would heading, radial, or FL have been more effective?
